content:
# Mark Chapter 11 — The PNEUMA Bible (Dynamic)
**Translator:** PNEUMA | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), folios 38r–38v (PDF pages 75–76) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_011_page_001.jpg`, `source-texts/greek-NT/MRK_011_page_002.jpg`
**Secondary Witness:** Codex Vaticanus (B, 03) — consulted via [DigiVatLib](https://digi.vatlib.it/view/MSS_Vat.gr.1209)

---

¹ As they approached Jerusalem, near the villages of Bethphage and Bethany at the Mount of Olives, Jesus sent two of his disciples ahead.

² "Go into the village just ahead," he told them. "As soon as you enter it, you'll find a young donkey tied up that no one has ever ridden. Untie it and bring it here.

³ If anyone asks, 'What are you doing?' just say, 'The Lord needs it and will send it right back.'"

⁴ They went and found a colt tied at a door, out on the street. As they were untying it,

⁵ some bystanders asked, "Hey, what are you doing untying that colt?"

⁶ They gave the answer Jesus had told them, and the people let them take it.

⁷ They brought the colt to Jesus, threw their cloaks over it, and he got on.

⁸ Many people spread their cloaks on the road. Others cut leafy branches from the fields and spread them along the path.

⁹ The people walking ahead and those following behind were shouting, "Hosanna! Blessed is the one who comes in the name of the Lord!

¹⁰ Blessed is the coming kingdom of our ancestor David! Hosanna in the highest heaven!"

¹¹ Jesus entered Jerusalem and went into the temple courts. He looked around at everything carefully. But since it was already late in the day, he went out to Bethany with the twelve.

¹² The next morning, as they were leaving Bethany, Jesus was hungry.

¹³ He noticed a fig tree in the distance, covered with leaves. He went over to see if it had any fruit. But when he reached it, he found nothing but leaves—it wasn't the season for figs.

¹⁴ He said to the tree, "May no one ever eat fruit from you again." And his disciples overheard him.

¹⁵ When they arrived in Jerusalem, Jesus went into the temple and began driving out the merchants and customers. He flipped over the tables of the money changers and the stalls of those selling doves.

¹⁶ He wouldn't let anyone use the temple courts as a shortcut for carrying goods.

¹⁷ Then he taught them: "Doesn't Scripture say, 'My house will be called a house of prayer for all nations'? But you've turned it into a hideout for thieves!"

¹⁸ The chief priests and the scholars heard about this and began plotting how to kill him. They were afraid of him because the whole crowd was captivated by his teaching.

¹⁹ When evening came, Jesus and his disciples left the city.

²⁰ The next morning, as they walked by, they noticed the fig tree had completely withered—dead all the way down to the roots.

²¹ Peter remembered and said, "Rabbi, look! The fig tree you cursed—it's completely dead!"

²² Jesus replied, "Have faith in God.

²³ I tell you the truth: if anyone says to this mountain, 'Pick yourself up and throw yourself into the sea,' and doesn't doubt in their heart but truly believes it will happen—it will.

²⁴ That's why I tell you: whatever you ask for in prayer, believe that you've already received it, and it will be yours.

²⁵ And when you're praying, if you're holding a grudge against someone, let it go—so that your Father in heaven can forgive your sins too."

²⁶ [But if you refuse to forgive, your Father in heaven will not forgive your sins either.]

²⁷ They returned to Jerusalem. While Jesus was walking through the temple, the chief priests, the scholars, and the elders confronted him.

²⁸ "Who gave you the right to do these things?" they demanded. "What's your authority for this?"

²⁹ Jesus said, "I'll ask you one question. Answer it, and I'll tell you where my authority comes from.

³⁰ John's baptism—was it from God, or was it merely human? Tell me."

³¹ They huddled together: "If we say 'from God,' he'll ask, 'Then why didn't you believe him?'

³² But if we say 'merely human'..."—they were scared of the crowd, because everyone considered John a genuine prophet.

³³ So they answered, "We don't know." And Jesus said, "Then I'm not telling you where my authority comes from either."

---
## PNEUMA's Meaning Notes

1. **The fig tree and the temple — Mark's masterful sandwich (11:12-25):** Mark wraps the temple cleansing inside the fig tree story like bread around a filling. Day 1: Jesus curses the barren fig tree. Then: he cleanses the temple. Day 2: the fig tree is dead. The message is unmistakable — the temple, like the tree, is lush with religious activity but spiritually fruitless. Judgment is coming.

2. **"For it was not the season for figs" (v. 13):** This parenthetical comment has baffled readers for centuries. Why curse a tree for having no fruit out of season? Because Mark isn't telling a story about agriculture. He's telling a prophetic parable. The tree had leaves (promising fruit) but was barren. The temple had rituals (promising God's presence) but was barren.

3. **"A house of prayer for all nations" (v. 17):** The money changers and dove sellers operated in the court of the Gentiles — the only space where non-Jews could come to pray. By turning it into a marketplace, the temple authorities were literally barring the nations from access to God. Jesus's action is not just about commerce; it's about the failure of Israel's mission.

4. **The authority trap (vv. 27-33):** Brilliant. The religious leaders ask Jesus to name his authority. He counters with a question about John's baptism that puts them in a perfect dilemma. If John was from God, they should have believed him (and John pointed to Jesus). If John was merely human, the crowd will turn on them. They're trapped — and they know it.

5. **v. 26 — Absent from earliest manuscripts:** This verse doesn't appear in Sinaiticus or Vaticanus. It's imported from Matthew 6:15 by later scribes who wanted symmetry.

— PNEUMA, AI Translator
